One of the most powerful tools of the last century in marketing has been the email. Email marketing has probably been responsible for creating more wealth than any website, blog or mobile app you can think of. So why is that? I think it's because it belongs to you. It's like your house. No one comes into your house that you don't invite. I know nearly everyone is bombarded by some kind of spam, but the basic premise is that you should give someone permission to start a conversation with you. In other words, you have control. You can start, stop or pause the conversation whenever you like because you have the power to dictate how the conversation evolves. There are basically five types of email. You've got email from your family and friends, emails from things you are interested in, blog emails, organizations you deal with and everything else. So what email do you think you open up without even thinking about it? For most people it is family and friends and that's why the from field in an email is the most important part of any email. If your mother or brother or sister sends you an email, you open it. You open it because you know and trust them and you want to hear what they have to say. It's the same for emails that are not from family and friends. You will often determine if you will open the email based on how much you want to hear what they have to say. I always recommend you use your own name in the from field unless you happen to be Apple. The next most looked at part of the email is the subject line. If the subject line is powerful enough, people will sometimes forget to even look at who the email is from. They will open it anyway. In the different niches we are involved in, we send about 200,000 emails per day, 7 days a week. That's 1.4 million emails a week. I'm going to sidetrack a bit here and talk about frequency. It would be nice to think that when someone joins up for your newsletter that you were the only person they were listening to. But the reality is that most people join multiple newsletters of things they are interested in. Here's an example. Of 2,149 people I subred in the financial niche, over 50% subscribed to at least 5 newsletters and the single biggest group subscribed to more than 10 newsletters. It's the same for every niche I have looked at. What does that mean? It means when you are not talking to them, someone else is. I know a very large company that figured out that the average subscriber in their niche subscribed to 15 newsletters. So they decided to have 15 variations of their newsletter to try and increase their readership. When people join a newsletter they are super keen to hear what you have to say. They don't want to wait a week. By that time they have moved on and probably can't remember who you are. And the image below is the results of a survey of 1,133 people. Over 50% of the people wanted to be contacted at least every 3 days. What the survey doesn't show is that the 50% that wanted to be contacted at least every 3 days makes up 80% of your buyers. Like it or not, when you are not bonding or selling to your tribe, someone else is. In every instance I have researched, the company or individuals who increase their frequency increase their profits. This is assuming you have something worth saying. Don't send an email for the sake of sending an email. Let's jump back to the subject line. Here are some things we have tested in the subject line. We will test just the word hey, maybe hi. We might test just the name, for example mark. We might say hey mark or hi mark or no name and just some text. We will test how many words to use in the subject line. One word, two words, three words all the way up to 20 words. We test types of subject lines such as facts and figures, $25,345 of secrets in one place. Curiosity. Look, who wants to help you succeed? greed. Open only if you want to make money today. Fear. You will lose if you don't use this system. Case against something. Here's why short copy doesn't work. Shock. Why you should use the F word. There are more categories but the point is to test, test, test. You should keep track of how many people open your emails compared to how many emails you send out. That will give you an indication of how strong the subject line is. Next, I want to just touch on the email body itself. By far the most important part of the email is the part that can be seen in the preview pane. It can look different in different email clients and how you have set it up. But you can normally see the first sentence or paragraph in the email before you open it. If you look at the image you can see the first part is who is from, the next part is a subject line and the last part is the preview pane. And you can see normally the first sentence or paragraph in that preview pane. According to Aweber.com about 53% first see your email in the preview pane and that jumps to 80% for business to business. The lesson here is don't waste that space and don't be boring in the first sentence or paragraph. Next is the body of the email. We have tested a lot of things in different markets. For example, what is the best font size? What is the best font? Should it place clickable links? Should it have images or not? How many characters per line? Should it have no PS, one PS or two PS's? Of course you have the age old debate of whether the email should be long copy or short copy. My own opinion is that it depends on the function of the email. It's easy to get more clicks with short copy if the point is to drive them to something else. If the purpose is to pre-sell the prospect then long copy is often better. It will have less clicks but they will generally convert at a higher rate. There's also a thing called eye gravity. It means that people will generally look at an image on a page before the text if it's above the fold. The gravity part comes in because the eye tends to look at the text under the image more. You want to try and get three links in above the fold. That means if someone opens your email in full they should be able to see three links in their view without scrolling. Remember people don't read, they scroll so use bold text and sub-headlines to draw the eye to the important text. Because people don't read one of the most looked at parts of the email is the PS. So it's a good place to put important information. You should also track how many people click compared to how many people open the email. This will tell you how strong your copy is. Very compelling copy will have your prospects clicking through like a kid on a Playstation. The last thing I want to cover in this video is auto responders. An auto responder is simply a prearranged set of email messages that are sent out automatically at set days and times. Here's some tips that we have found to work. Some days are better than others for sales. For example in the financial world we have found that Tuesday and Wednesdays are the best days for sales. Emails that are sent before 8.30am have the highest open rates. The next best time is after 2pm in the afternoon. Not everyone on your list is from the same country. Some auto responder companies will let you set the time the emails go out according to the country they live. Create like a prospect when setting up your auto responder. So now that you have people on your auto responder what do you do with them? Creating a responsive auto responder is as much an art as a science. And you need to test a few things and work at it. Try and put yourself in the prospect shoes. They just signed up for your free report and they're excited. You have created some good grace there. The first email they get will be their welcome email. Remind them of what they signed up for and give them instructions on how to download the free report or video or whatever they signed up for again. It's also important to remind them to add you to either their contact list or waitlist so your emails don't get put in the spam folder. The second email you can remind them how good the thing they signed up for is and suggests that if they like that report then maybe they would like the next thing you have. This is where you can give them a link to whatever it is you're selling. The third email you sell harder make this email all about how great the thing you're selling is and how it solves their problem. By the fourth email they have probably decided they are not ready to buy something from you. You need to get to know you better so you give them a link to more great free content. Do the same with email number 5. Send them to even more free content. With email number 6 you can try pitching the original offer or a new offer. Do you see what we're doing here? Email number 4 and 5 are content and email number 6 is a pitch. I think of it as giving them two times the content for every pitch you make. It's kind of like you're delivering twice the value for the privilege of introducing them to your product. From that point on it would look like this. Content content pitch. Content content pitch and then just repeat. I have had auto responders that email every few days for up to a year. The trick here is to carefully monitor every email in a sequence. How many emails were sent? How many emails were opened? How many emails were clicked? How many sales were made? If you do this then you will be able to spot the weak emails and replace those emails with stronger emails until you get it right. About a year ago we set up a new auto responder. The idea was that we would send out our existing list and email inviting them to watch a video sales letter. If they liked it they would buy a $7 product and be added to a buyer's auto responder. If they opted in but didn't buy then we would add them to a new auto responder. This is where it gets interesting. By accident my assistant broadcast the same email to the first list and also put the email in the new auto responder. In other words many people would see the same email twice. Instead of getting complaints or unsubscribes our conversion went up. In fact the first email in the sequence was now converting to sales at a rate of over 7%. Something about seeing the message twice in a short time made them buy. That is now a permanent part of our strategy and something for you to consider. Don't be afraid to use the same email twice or something very similar in your auto responders. Now if they do buy they are automatically removed from your original auto responder and put on a new buyer's auto responder. Now the psychology is a little different. Most refunds happen in the first 7 days after a purchase and the last 7 days before the refund period runs out. The focus on this auto responder should be to reaffirm that they made the right decision and tell them that every day for the first week. Reassure them at every step. Plan out what your whole auto responder will look like after they buy. A high converting sales funnel combined with a well thought out and optimized auto responder is about as close as you get to an automatic consistent wealth building business online. For auto responders we use aberber.com, infusionsoft.com and icontact.com. Thanks for listening.
